prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the bicycle's popularity in the 1890s depended on

choices.text: earlier technical improvements such as pedals, a chain, and air-filled tires
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage links each improvement to new riders before the craze.

choices.text: the original 1817 design being kept almost entirely unchanged
choices.rationale: The design changed substantially from 1817.

choices.text: a broad return to horse-drawn travel after a brief period of decline
choices.rationale: The bicycle's appeal was needing no horse.

choices.text: the complete disappearance of nearly all roads across the countryside
choices.rationale: Roads did not disappear; rough roads were a problem.

explanation: Popularity followed the accumulation of improvements.

stimulus.title: The Machine That Walked
stimulus.text: The earliest ancestor of the bicycle had no pedals at all. Built in 1817, it was a wooden frame on two wheels that a rider straddled and pushed along with the feet, like walking while seated. People mocked it, and rutted roads made it impractical, so it soon faded. Decades passed before inventors added pedals, then a chain, then air-filled tires that smoothed the ride. Each change drew new riders, and by the 1890s the bicycle had become a craze. It offered something rare for the time: cheap, independent travel that needed no horse and no rails. For many people, especially those long denied easy movement, the machine quietly widened the boundaries of daily life.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that farmers near volcanoes

choices.text: have no idea that the nearby volcano could ever erupt at all
choices.rationale: They 'weigh' the danger, so they are aware of it.

choices.text: manage to harvest crops only in the years without any eruption
choices.rationale: No such yearly pattern is stated.

choices.text: would actually rather farm poor soil than the rich volcanic soil
choices.rationale: They value the rich soil, not poor soil.

choices.text: accept a known risk in exchange for unusually productive land
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage describes weighing the gift of soil against the danger.

explanation: The 'uneasy bargain' framing implies an accepted, known risk.

stimulus.title: Gifts of the Volcano
stimulus.text: It seems strange that people would farm in the shadow of a volcano, but many do, and for good reason. When a volcano erupts, it scatters ash rich in minerals such as potassium and phosphorus. Over years, this ash breaks down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. Crops planted in it can grow unusually well, which is why slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages. The bargain is uneasy: the same mountain that feeds the fields can also threaten them. Farmers weigh the steady gift of rich soil against the rare but real danger overhead, and generation after generation, many choose to stay.

prompt: The passage suggests that the link between sleep and performance is

choices.text: strongest at a moderate amount rather than at either extreme
choices.rationale: Correct. The 'curve' detail implies a moderate optimum.

choices.text: exactly the same for every person regardless of timing or amount
choices.rationale: It mentions 'the right times,' implying timing matters.

choices.text: strongest when a person sleeps as little as they possibly can
choices.rationale: Too little sleep is linked to poorer performance.

choices.text: essentially unrelated to how well a person remembers things
choices.rationale: The passage ties sleep to memory consolidation.

explanation: The curve description implies a middle optimum, not an extreme.

stimulus.title: The Night Shift of the Brain
stimulus.text: Sleep can look like idleness, but the brain is busy. During deep sleep, waves of electrical activity sweep across the cortex, and researchers think these waves help move the day's memories into longer-term storage. In one line of experiments, students who slept after studying a list of words remembered more of them than students who stayed awake the same number of hours. Sleep may also clear away molecular waste that builds up while we are awake. None of this means more sleep is always better; the relationship appears to follow a curve, with both too little and too much linked to poorer performance. What the brain seems to need is enough, at the right times.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that a sparrow's ability to learn a full song depends on

choices.text: hearing adult song during a brief window early in its life
choices.rationale: Correct. A bird that hears no song in that window sings only a thin tune.

choices.text: the age of any sound recordings made nearby in past decades
choices.rationale: Old recordings illustrate drift, not a bird's learning ability.

choices.text: the particular color and pattern of the feathers on its crown
choices.rationale: Crown color is not linked to learning.

choices.text: the overall size of the meadow in which it happens to live
choices.rationale: Meadow size is never mentioned as a factor.

explanation: The passage ties full song to exposure during the early learning window.

stimulus.title: Songs With Accents
stimulus.text: A white-crowned sparrow raised near San Francisco does not sing quite like one raised a hundred miles north. The basic melody is the same, but small flourishes differ from place to place, forming what biologists call song dialects. Young sparrows learn these dialects by listening to adult males during a brief window in their first months. A bird that hears no song in that window grows up to sing a thin, simplified tune. The dialects can drift over generations, much as human accents do, so a recording made decades ago may sound subtly foreign to the birds singing in the same meadow today.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that Nadia almost refused the loaner because she

choices.text: felt unworthy of playing such a fine and valuable instrument
choices.rationale: Correct. 'It felt wrong to play something so fine' signals a sense of unworthiness.

choices.text: strongly disliked the pieces she was assigned for the recital
choices.rationale: Her feelings about the pieces are not the reason given.

choices.text: actually preferred instruments whose tuning pegs kept slipping
choices.rationale: She struggled with slipping pegs; she did not prefer them.

choices.text: had never learned how to read the music set before her
choices.rationale: Nothing suggests she cannot read music.

explanation: Her hesitation stems from feeling the instrument was too fine for her.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the 'tun' state is best described as a form of

choices.text: suspended dormancy from which the animal can later revive
choices.rationale: Correct. The animal halts activity yet can revive, indicating dormancy.

choices.text: social cooperation among many tardigrades at once
choices.rationale: No social behavior is described.

choices.text: permanent death that ends the animal's life for good
choices.rationale: It can be revived, so it is not dead.

choices.text: rapid growth that lets the animal enlarge under stress
choices.rationale: Drying into a husk is not growth.

explanation: Because the creature can be revived after years, the tun is a dormant, not lethal, state.

stimulus.title: The Survivor
stimulus.text: Smaller than a grain of salt, the tardigrade looks almost comical, plodding on eight stubby legs. Its talent, however, is anything but ordinary. When its surroundings dry out, the creature expels most of its water and curls into a husk called a tun. In this state it can endure boiling heat, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill nearly any other animal. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it. Scientists are still untangling how the tardigrade protects its cells, but one clue is a special set of proteins that seem to harden into glass, cradling delicate structures until water returns.

prompt: The passage most strongly suggests that, before Gutenberg, identical copies of a text were

choices.text: rare, because copying by hand was slow and introduced small variations
choices.rationale: Correct. Hand-copying was slow and the passage stresses the novelty of identical printed copies.

choices.text: widely dismissed as worthless by the scholars of the period
choices.rationale: Scholars are said to prize handwritten books, not scorn them.

choices.text: common, since nearly every European town had its own busy copyists
choices.rationale: The passage implies the opposite of commonness.

choices.text: produced even faster by hand than printed copies later would be
choices.rationale: Hand-copying took a year, far slower than printing.

explanation: The contrast the passage draws implies hand-copied texts were neither fast nor uniform.

stimulus.title: Letters of Lead
stimulus.text: Before the 1450s, a book in Europe was copied by hand, a task that could occupy a scribe for a year. Johannes Gutenberg changed that arithmetic. By casting individual letters in metal, he could arrange them into a page, print hundreds of copies, then rearrange the same letters for the next page. The cost of a book did not fall overnight, and many scholars still prized handwritten volumes. But within fifty years, presses across the continent had produced millions of books. Ideas that once crept from city to city now traveled in bulk, and a reader in one town could hold the exact words read by a stranger far away.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred from the passage that the octopus's color change is

choices.text: triggered by the temperature of the surrounding seawater
choices.rationale: Temperature is never mentioned as a cause.

choices.text: produced by muscles widening sacs of pigment beneath the skin
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage states muscles widen the sacs to reveal color.

choices.text: perceptible to the octopus itself but not to other animals
choices.rationale: The octopus is colorblind, so it cannot perceive its own colors.

choices.text: learned only gradually over the course of the animal's life
choices.rationale: The change happens in under a second, not slowly over a lifetime.

explanation: The text directly ties color change to muscles pulling on pigment sacs.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.
